      Don=E2=80=99t throw out your computers
   =20

Well here=E2=80=99s my new financial heist movie script. A big bank has a l=
ot of computers that keep track of its customers=E2=80=99 accounts. It peri=
odically buys new computers to do a better job of keeping track of those ac=
counts. When it does that, it hires a moving company to cart away all of th=
e old computers. The moving company makes a few extra bucks by selling the =
old computer hardware to, I don=E2=80=99t know, scrappy small technology bu=
sinesses that are happy to have the bank=E2=80=99s slightly outdated hardwa=
re. Ideally the bank, or the moving company, would delete all the data on t=
he computers first, but that takes time and time is money and sometimes the=
y forget.
So what you do is, you set up a scrappy small tech business as a cover, and=
 you go to the bank=E2=80=99s moving company=E2=80=99s computer sale and bu=
y all the computers, and then you turn them on and get all of the bank=E2=
=80=99s customers=E2=80=99 account information, and then you steal=C2=A0the=
ir money. Okay, having written this all out, I guess=C2=A0it is a boring he=
ist movie, never mind.  Still:

The Securities and Exchange Commission today announced charges against Morg=
an Stanley Smith Barney LLC (MSSB) stemming from the firm=E2=80=99s extensi=
ve failures, over a five-year period, to protect the personal identifying i=
nformation, or PII, of approximately 15 million customers. MSSB has agreed =
to pay a $35 million penalty to settle the SEC charges.
The SEC=E2=80=99s order finds that, as far back as 2015, MSSB failed to pro=
perly dispose of devices containing its customers=E2=80=99 PII. On multiple=
 occasions, MSSB hired a moving and storage company with no experience or e=
xpertise in data destruction services to decommission thousands of hard dri=
ves and servers containing the PII of millions of its customers. Moreover, =
according to the SEC=E2=80=99s order, over several years, MSSB failed to pr=
operly monitor the moving company=E2=80=99s work. The staff=E2=80=99s inves=
tigation found that the moving company sold to a third party thousands of M=
SSB devices including servers and hard drives, some of which contained cust=
omer PII, and which were eventually resold on an internet auction site with=
out removal of such customer PII. While MSSB recovered some of the devices,=
 which were shown to contain thousands of pieces of unencrypted customer da=
ta, the firm has not recovered the vast majority of the devices.

Great stuff. If you find a 2016-vintage Morgan Stanley computer on EBay and=
 crack it open to find customer information, I guess you can =E2=80=A6=C2=
=A0do =E2=80=A6=C2=A0something with that? I don=E2=80=99t know. Here, from =
 the SEC complaint, is what one guy did:
On October 25, 2017, nearly a year after the completion of the 2016 Data Ce=
nter Decommissioning, MSSB received an email from an IT consultant in Oklah=
oma (=E2=80=9CConsultant=E2=80=9D). In that email, Consultant informed MSSB=
 that he had purchased hard drives from an online auction site and that he =
had access to MSSB=E2=80=99s data on those devices. In that email, Consulta=
nt informed MSSB that =E2=80=9C[y]ou are a major financial institution and =
should be following some very stringent guidelines on how to deal with reti=
ring hardware. Or at the very least getting some kind of verification of da=
ta destruction from the vendors you sell equipment to.=E2=80=9D MSSB eventu=
ally repurchased the hard drives in Consultant=E2=80=99s possession.
Somehow the SEC neglects to mention how much Morgan Stanley paid him for th=
ose hard drives. How much would you charge Morgan Stanley for the hard driv=
es in this situation? I do not want to give you legal advice, and I think t=
hat you would want to get some legal advice before trying this, but I think=
 if you offered these hard drives to Morgan Stanley for, say, $100,000 each=
, they might pay you? Is there a bug bounty program for, you know, you thre=
w out the wrong hard drive? They paid the SEC $35 million; there is money i=
n the budget for this sort of thing.
The rest of the complaint is full of suggestive hints at the scale of Morga=
n Stanley=E2=80=99s after-the-fact garbage hunt:

In late 2017, MSSB launched an investigation into the disposition of the de=
vices that were part of the 2016 Data Center Decommissioning project and de=
termined that Moving Company had also delivered the 8,000 back-up tapes rem=
oved from one of the data centers to IT Corp B. MSSB emailed IT Corp B on J=
anuary 19, 2018 asking whether IT Corp B could =E2=80=9Cconfirm the disposi=
tion of =E2=80=A63k lbs of tapes.=E2=80=9D IT Corp B responded: =E2=80=9CI =
can confirm that we did send this load of tapes for secure waste to energy =
incineration. Although that lot # is not the lot # we used. They were proce=
ssed =E2=80=98Confidential Material=E2=80=99 in June of 2016.=E2=80=9D MSSB=
=E2=80=99s basis for believing that these tapes were in fact destroyed with=
out any unauthorized access to customer PII and consumer report information=
 hinges on this email. MSSB has no other verification or 5 documentation th=
at these tapes were destroyed.
In June 2021, MSSB obtained another fourteen of the missing hard drives fro=
m a downstream purchaser. Based on forensic analysis of these hard drives, =
thirteen of the devices contained a total of at least 140,000 pieces of cus=
tomer PII. The vast majority of the hard drives from the 2016 Data Center D=
ecommissioning remain missing. =E2=80=A6
MSSB has identified an international shipping project that may have involve=
d Moving Company. MSSB can state only that =E2=80=9Cdocuments suggest=E2=80=
=9D that Moving Company transported 18-36 unspecified devices to a storage =
location in New York City. It was contemplated that those devices would be =
shipped internationally to Europe, potentially by Moving Company.=C2=A0

Possibly thousands of ancient Morgan Stanley storage devices, all over the =
globe, possibly full of customer data, possibly useful for nefarious purpos=
es. Probably not; there is no suggestion that anyone did anything at all ne=
farious with any of these things, and also not much suggestion that you=C2=
=A0could=C2=A0do anything particularly bad. (You=E2=80=99re not supposed to=
 hand out customers=E2=80=99=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cpersonal identifying information=
=E2=80=9D to random hackers, but there=E2=80=99s no suggestion that it was,=
 like, account passwords.)=C2=A0
In recent years it has been popular for investment bank executives to say t=
hat they were becoming tech companies: They were hiring developers, buildin=
g apps, talking about big data. This is a good thing to say, I suppose, ins=
ofar as being a tech company means that you can make large profits without =
putting too much of your own balance sheet at risk, that you can scale rapi=
dly, that you can compete with Silicon Valley for employees.
But there is also an old-school understanding of investment banking in whic=
h it is a business of implicit knowledge, of personal connections, that the=
 value that a good banker can add can=E2=80=99t be reduced to an algorithm.=
 There is a tension there. If banks are tech companies then they will inves=
t in good consistent electronic communication tools that create good search=
able records. If banking is a business of personal connections, then banker=
s will ignore those tools and text clients on their personal cell phones be=
cause=C2=A0that feels more personal. Morgan Stanley   got fined $200 millio=
n for that this summer. You are supposed to use the official communication =
tools.
Similarly, if banking is a business of implicit knowledge, go ahead and thr=
ow out the old hard drives; what=E2=80=99s important is the knowledge in th=
e hearts and minds of the bankers. If banking is a data business, you shoul=
d probably wipe the hard drives before you throw them out.

      SPAC SPAC SPAC: Chamath
   =20
 =20

A special purpose acquisition company is a gamble by its sponsors. The way =
a SPAC works is that the sponsors=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0often a private equit=
y firm, but sometimes just a handful of rich individuals =E2=80=94 spend=C2=
=A0a few million dollars of their own money to pay some startup costs for t=
he SPAC. These fees pay for underwriters, lawyers, accountants, registratio=
n fees, etc. And then the SPAC raises a few hundred million dollars from pu=
blic investors, and the sponsors have two years to=C2=A0find a company to t=
ake public with that money. If they find a target, and the public investors=
 approve the deal, the sponsors generally get rich. In rough numbers they g=
et shares of the target company worth about 25% of the hundreds of millions=
 of dollars that they raised from the public. Their=C2=A0early investment o=
f a few million dollars can turn into hundreds of millions of dollars of pu=
blic-company stock. (Generally if they find a deal the SPAC=E2=80=99s spons=
ors will invest more of their own money, so it=E2=80=99s not quite this luc=
rative, but that is the essential nature of the gamble.)=C2=A0It is helpful=
 if they find a good target and the stock goes up, but the main thing is th=
at if they do a deal at all they get amply paid. If they don=E2=80=99t, the=
y give the public investors their money back and lose their startup investm=
ent.
The payoff there is incredibly asymmetric, and so for a while in 2020 and e=
arly 2021 everyone with a reputation and a few million dollars to spare pil=
ed into the SPAC business.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CYou and some buddies put up $500,0=
00 each and then you get back $20 million each=E2=80=9D was a not entirely =
inaccurate pitch for sponsoring a SPAC. This led to a SPAC glut: There were=
 more SPACs than good deals, the market got pickier, and now it is 2022 and=
 a lot of SPACs look likely to reach their two-year deadline without a deal=
. Their sponsors will lose their wagers.
The King of SPACs was (is?) Chamath Palihapitiya, the founder of Social Cap=
ital.=C2=A0He was early to the boom: He launched a SPAC with the clunky nam=
e Social Capital Hedosophia Holdings Corp., and the punchy ticker IPOA,
  [1]
=C2=A0back in 2017. IPOA  raised $600 million and used it to do a merger wi=
th space company Virgin Galactic Holdings Inc.; Social Capital started by p=
utting about $12 million into the SPAC to cover startup costs, and ended up=
 with hundreds of millions of dollars of stock. (Palihapitiya   cashed in $=
315 million worth earlier this year.) Virgin closed yesterday at $5.29, dow=
n about 47% from the $10 price at which IPOA raised money, so if you were i=
n this SPAC from the beginning it was not exactly a=C2=A0good=C2=A0deal. Bu=
t it was=C2=A0a=C2=A0deal, and=C2=A0Palihapitiya=C2=A0made a lot of money f=
rom it.
Also to be fair for a while it looked like a good deal =E2=80=94=C2=A0Virgi=
n closed as high as $59.41 last February =E2=80=94=C2=A0and Palihapitiya us=
ed that success to raise more and bigger SPACs. IPOB, IPOC, IPOD, IPOE, IPO=
F, etc.
  [2]
; IPOF =E2=80=94=C2=A0Social Capital Hedosophia Holdings Corp. VI =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0raised $1.15 billion in October 2020. The sponsor =E2=80=94=C2=A0Soci=
al Capital,=C2=A0Palihapitiya=E2=80=99s firm =E2=80=94=C2=A0put about $22 m=
illion into that one for startup costs. If it had closed a deal, it would h=
ave paid off hundreds of millions of dollars. It did not. Palihapitiya  ann=
ounced today on his Substack:

Today, we started the process of winding down IPOD and IPOF.
This means that the funds raised by IPOD and IPOF will be returned to their=
 respective shareholders. Over the past two years, we evaluated more than 1=
00 targets and while we came close to doing a deal several times, we ultima=
tely walked away each time for a couple of reasons:
Valuation. A combination of factors made it very difficult to find a compan=
y at a reasonable valuation and margin of safety. Ultimately, to get a deal=
 done would have required us stretching on price or buying an inferior asse=
t =E2=80=93 neither were things we felt comfortable doing.
Volatility. We saw resistance from management teams who either weren=E2=80=
=99t prepared for or didn=E2=80=99t want to face the public markets in the =
face of current volatility.

I cannot fault this. Each time he launched a SPAC, Palihapitiya bet $20 mil=
lion-ish to win $300 million-ish. The bets kept paying off, so he kept play=
ing. If all of those bets had paid off =E2=80=94=C2=A0if he had launched on=
ly SPACs that actually ended up finding deals =E2=80=94=C2=A0then that woul=
d have been way too conservative. These were very high expected value bets,=
 so he made a lot of them. Some of them didn=E2=80=99t work out, but  all i=
n all he did great:
The founder and CEO of Social Capital Holdings Inc. said in an interview th=
at the company has made about $750 million in SPACs, roughly doubling its m=
oney. The gains come from the six deals it completed such as SoFi. SPAC cre=
ators are protected from big losses through lucrative incentives. Social Ca=
pital creates its SPACs by partnering with other investment firms.=C2=A0
Bloomberg   reported in August:
All five of his SPACs that merged with acquisition targets are now trading =
well below their starting price of $10. Some, like Virgin Galactic Holdings=
 Inc., are down more than 25%. Taken from its peak price, back in February =
2021, when Palihapitiya was tweeting things like =E2=80=9Ctrust the process=
=E2=80=9D with a screenshot of his SPAC returns, the stock is down 88%.=C2=
=A0
Palihapitiya was for a time an advocate for SPACs as a way to democratize i=
nitial public offerings and bring disruptive new companies to the markets =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9CIPO 2.0,=E2=80=9D he called it =E2=80=94=C2=A0and I=
 suppose you could quibble with the results there. But as a feat of market =
timing it is hard to argue with his track record.=C2=A0

      SPAC SPAC SPAC: DWAC
   =20
 =20

A special purpose acquisition company is a gamble by its sponsors, etc.=C2=
=A0The payoff is incredibly asymmetric, and so for a while in late 2020 and=
 early 2021 everyone with a reputation and a few million dollars to spare p=
iled into the SPAC business. This included a lot of random rich people who =
saw their friends making money and wanted in on the action. And then there =
was a SPAC glut, deals got scarce, and some number of SPAC sponsors are goi=
ng to lose their wagers.=C2=A0We have  talked about this before, and I conf=
ess that I find it kind of funny: Late-in-the-boom SPACs are the rare finan=
cial innovation that worked out fine for ordinary investors (who get their =
money back with interest) but fleeced=C2=A0rich well-connected insiders.
One weird result of this is that if you are a late-in-the-boom SPAC sponsor=
 you are probably rich but maybe not=C2=A0that=C2=A0rich, and while the pay=
off of a SPAC is very asymmetric, (1) it looks increasingly distant and (2)=
 you don=E2=80=99t want to throw good money after bad. And SPACs have prett=
y thin balance sheets=C2=A0given their potential riches. So we have talked =
recently about Digital World Acquisition Corp., the $293 million SPAC that =
has signed a deal to take Trump Media & Technology Group public. DWAC=E2=80=
=99s sponsors put up about $11.3 million to start the SPAC, and if the deal=
 closes their stake could be worth=C2=A0about $165 million. But the deal is=
 still in limbo, due mostly to the US Securities and Exchange Commission=E2=
=80=99s review of the proxy statement for the merger,=C2=A0and the sponsors=
 had to put up another $2.9 million for a three-month extension, and they w=
aited until the absolute last minute to do that. On the one hand betting $2=
.9 million to win $165 million seems like a good deal. On the other hand th=
e odds aren=E2=80=99t great, money might be a bit tight, and every penny co=
mes out of the sponsors=E2=80=99 own pockets.
Anyway  this made me laugh:

Executives behind a blank-cheque company that plans to take Donald Trump=E2=
=80=99s media business public have failed to pay their proxy solicitors eve=
n as they struggle to drum up support for an extension to complete the deal=
.
Digital World Acquisition Corporation, a special purpose acquisition compan=
y set up by Patrick Orlando, has not paid Saratoga Proxy Consulting for its=
 work helping to rally shareholders, according to people familiar with the =
situation.
DWAC owes the New York-based firm a six-figure sum but=C2=A0Orlando has inf=
ormed it that there is no money to pay the bill, one of the people said. On=
 Friday, the company announced that it had brought on a new proxy solicitor=
, Alliance Advisors. =E2=80=A6
Despite an aggressive campaign by Orlando to reach DWAC=E2=80=99s base of r=
etail investors, just over 40 per cent have voted in favour of the extensio=
n as of this week, according to a source familiar with the count. The compa=
ny requires 65 per cent of shareholders to approve extending the deadline a=
t its meeting on October 10.
Retail investors are notoriously difficult to reach and companies can often=
 end up spending millions of dollars soliciting their votes.

Yeah I mean on the one hand you really do want those votes; spending a few =
million dollars on proxy soliciting to make $165 million feels like a good =
deal. On the other hand:
=09Even if you can drum up the retail votes for the extension, there is no =
guarantee of the deal actually closing;=C2=A0=09That proxy-solicitor money =
comes out of your own pocket; and=09Given that they only got 40% of the sha=
reholders to vote, it=E2=80=99s not totally clear that Saratoga exactly ear=
ned its fees.

In other DWAC news, its PIPE =E2=80=94=C2=A0the $1 billion   private invest=
ment in public equity=C2=A0that it raised from some investors late last yea=
r =E2=80=94=C2=A0is due to expire today. The New York Times  reported last =
week:

Representatives for several investors in the PIPE agreement, all of whom de=
clined to be identified because the deal is still pending, said they had no=
t decided what to do if the agreement expired, but at least a few were ambi=
valent about remaining in the deal.
The investor representatives said neither Digital World nor E.F. Hutton, th=
e investment bank that arranged the financing, had offered any guidance. On=
e investor responded to a question about the status of the financing deal w=
ith an emoji of a person shrugging.

We have talked about that PIPE before, and it seems like an   unbelievably =
good deal for its investors, if the deal ever closes. If the deal doesn=E2=
=80=99t close then it costs those investors nothing but the time and aggrav=
ation of dealing with DWAC and Trump Media & Technology Group. Which is may=
be too much to pay, given the current odds.

   =20
      IPO 1.0
   =20
 =20

Here=E2=80=99s a story about Instacart Inc.=E2=80=99s=C2=A0planned initial =
public offering:

In meetings with prospective investors in recent weeks, Instacart executive=
s said they didn=E2=80=99t plan to issue many new shares in their IPO, the =
people said. The sale of mostly employee shares would allow Instacart=E2=80=
=99s staff, including some of its earliest hires, to at last cash out of so=
me of the shares they have been accumulating. =E2=80=A6
While Instacart will sell a small percentage of new shares, the bulk of its=
 offering will come from employee shares that will be sold directly to new =
investors at an agreed-upon price ahead of a stock-market debut. Details of=
 the listing could change depending on market conditions and other factors.
Instacart had previously leaned toward going public through a direct listin=
g, The Wall Street Journal previously reported.
In a direct listing, a company=E2=80=99s shares simply start trading on an =
exchange on a set day. There is a reference price for where trading could s=
tart, but no shares are sold in advance at that price. Existing shareholder=
s can sell their shares, but companies don=E2=80=99t raise any cash by goin=
g public.

Yeah two years ago this would have been a direct listing. =E2=80=9CYour emp=
loyees can just sell stock on the stock exchange without a big coordinated =
process; they=E2=80=99ll get the market-clearing price rather than having t=
o sell at a discount so big institutions can get an IPO pop.=E2=80=9D Now i=
t=E2=80=99s a normal IPO.
One possible story here is that direct listings and SPACs are solutions to =
the bull-market problem that   IPOs mostly go up. If you price an IPO at $2=
0 and the stock trades up to $30 in a day, you =E2=80=9Cleft money on the t=
able.=E2=80=9D If every week 10 companies price IPOs that trade up 50%, and=
 you want to go public, you will start thinking about ways to avoid leaving=
 money on the table. Selling stock in a direct listing =E2=80=94=C2=A0where=
 you get the market-clearing price in an auction, not a price negotiated wi=
th your underwriters based on stated demand from big institutions =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0is a way to do that. A   SPAC is not, particularly, but it was market=
ed that way anyway.
But when the stock market is down and volatility is up, SPACs and direct li=
stings are less appealing. The IPO pop =E2=80=94=C2=A0the propensity of mos=
t IPOs to trade up on the first day =E2=80=94=C2=A0pays for certainty. =E2=
=80=9CWe bring you IPOs every week and they usually go up,=E2=80=9D banks i=
mplicitly say to their investor clients, =E2=80=9Cso if you like that servi=
ce we expect you to buy the IPOs that don=E2=80=99t go up too.=E2=80=9D If =
you sell stock in a direct listing, nobody has to buy it. If you sell stock=
 in an IPO, they =E2=80=A6=C2=A0well, nobody=C2=A0has=C2=A0to buy it, but i=
t is encouraged.
  [3]
=C2=A0In a down market you don=E2=80=99t have to worry about the problem of=
 IPOs going up too much; you have to worry about actually getting your mone=
y. The IPO might be a better way of doing that than the alternatives.

      It=E2=80=99s fun to have fun
   =20
 =20

Aww  this is sweet:

Amid the wreckage of the great bond-market bust of 2022, there is a silver =
lining for some: Traders in the trenches of the $24 trillion Treasury marke=
t say it=E2=80=99s finally exciting again. ...
Of course in finance, the levels of excitement is usually tightly correlate=
d with the amount of money being made. So it=E2=80=99s no surprise the two =
are rising in tandem. Trading revenues have boomed for the major US banks w=
ith a strong presence in US rates markets. At Citadel Securities, an import=
ant provider of prices in the Treasury market, this year=E2=80=99s turbulen=
ce led to a record $4.2 billion in first-half net trading revenue.=C2=A0
=E2=80=9CWe are right in the sweet spot of rates really being an interestin=
g market, with clients being excited to trade,=E2=80=9D said Paul Hamill, h=
ead of global fixed income, currencies and commodities distribution at Cita=
del Securities. =E2=80=9CEveryone is spending all day talking to clients an=
d talking to each other. It=E2=80=99s been fun.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0

I recommend taking that quote as literally as possible. The head of fixed i=
ncome at Citadel Securities is like =E2=80=9Coh I get to talk to my friends=
 all day, it=E2=80=99s so fun.=E2=80=9D How nice for him.=C2=A0
We have spent years around here talking about how people were worried about=
 bond market liquidity: People who wanted to buy and sell bonds had a hard =
time doing it, because banks and other market makers were not willing to co=
mmit a lot of capital. This worry was often expressed as a hypothetical: Su=
re, things look fine now because the market is quiet, but in times of volat=
ility the lack of liquidity will be a problem. But of course times of volat=
ility are when market makers can make a lot of money. Which makes them more=
 willing to commit capital, because they are having fun.

<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Don=E2=80=99t throw out your computers</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Well here=E2=80=99s my new financial heist mov=
ie script. A big bank has a lot of computers that keep track of its custome=
rs=E2=80=99 accounts. It periodically buys new computers to do a better job=
 of keeping track of those accounts. When it does that, it hires a moving c=
ompany to cart away all of the old computers. The moving company makes a fe=
w extra bucks by selling the old computer hardware to, I don=E2=80=99t know=
, scrappy small technology businesses that are happy to have the bank=E2=80=
=99s slightly outdated hardware. Ideally the bank, or the moving company, w=
ould delete all the data on the computers first, but that takes time and ti=
me is money and sometimes they forget.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So what you do is, you set up a scrappy small =
tech business as a cover, and you go to the bank=E2=80=99s moving company=
=E2=80=99s computer sale and buy all the computers, and then you turn them =
on and get all of the bank=E2=80=99s customers=E2=80=99 account information=
, and then you steal=C2=A0their money. Okay, having written this all out, I=
 guess=C2=A0it is a boring heist movie, never mind. <a href=3D"https://link=
.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29111652.284535/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvd=
i9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMi0xNjg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBc66b14d2" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;"> Still</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Securities and Exchange Commission today a=
nnounced charges against Morgan Stanley Smith Barney LLC (MSSB) stemming fr=
om the firm=E2=80=99s extensive failures, over a five-year period, to prote=
ct the personal identifying information, or PII, of approximately 15 millio=
n customers. MSSB has agreed to pay a $35 million penalty to settle the SEC=
 charges.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The SEC=E2=80=99s order finds that, as far bac=
k as 2015, MSSB failed to properly dispose of devices containing its custom=
ers=E2=80=99 PII. On multiple occasions, MSSB hired a moving and storage co=
mpany with no experience or expertise in data destruction services to decom=
mission thousands of hard drives and servers containing the PII of millions=
 of its customers. Moreover, according to the SEC=E2=80=99s order, over sev=
eral years, MSSB failed to properly monitor the moving company=E2=80=99s wo=
rk. The staff=E2=80=99s investigation found that the moving company sold to=
 a third party thousands of MSSB devices including servers and hard drives,=
 some of which contained customer PII, and which were eventually resold on =
an internet auction site without removal of such customer PII. While MSSB r=
ecovered some of the devices, which were shown to contain thousands of piec=
es of unencrypted customer data, the firm has not recovered the vast majori=
ty of the devices.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Great stuff. If you find a 2016-vintage Morgan=
 Stanley computer on EBay and crack it open to find customer information, I=
 guess you can =E2=80=A6=C2=A0do =E2=80=A6=C2=A0something with that? I don=
=E2=80=99t know. Here, from <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.=
com/click/29111652.284535/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9saXRpZ2F0aW9uL2FkbWluL=
zIwMjIvMzQtOTU4MzIucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB47a0dd36" itemprop=3D"Stor=
yLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; t=
ext-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-co=
lor: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> the SEC=
 complaint</a>, is what one guy did:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On October 25, 2017, nearly a year=
 after the completion of the 2016 Data Center Decommissioning, MSSB receive=
d an email from an IT consultant in Oklahoma (=E2=80=9CConsultant=E2=80=9D)=
. In that email, Consultant informed MSSB that he had purchased hard drives=
 from an online auction site and that he had access to MSSB=E2=80=99s data =
on those devices. In that email, Consultant informed MSSB that =E2=80=9C[y]=
ou are a major financial institution and should be following some very stri=
ngent guidelines on how to deal with retiring hardware. Or at the very leas=
t getting some kind of verification of data destruction from the vendors yo=
u sell equipment to.=E2=80=9D MSSB eventually repurchased the hard drives i=
n Consultant=E2=80=99s possession.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Somehow the SEC neglects to mention how much M=
organ Stanley paid him for those hard drives. How much would you charge Mor=
gan Stanley for the hard drives in this situation? I do not want to give yo=
u legal advice, and I think that you would want to get some legal advice be=
fore trying this, but I think if you offered these hard drives to Morgan St=
anley for, say, $100,000 each, they might pay you? Is there a bug bounty pr=
ogram for, you know, you threw out the wrong hard drive? They paid the SEC =
$35 million; there is money in the budget for this sort of thing.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The rest of the complaint is full of suggestiv=
e hints at the scale of Morgan Stanley=E2=80=99s after-the-fact garbage hun=
t:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In late 2017, MSSB launched an investigation i=
nto the disposition of the devices that were part of the 2016 Data Center D=
ecommissioning project and determined that Moving Company had also delivere=
d the 8,000 back-up tapes removed from one of the data centers to IT Corp B=
. MSSB emailed IT Corp B on January 19, 2018 asking whether IT Corp B could=
 =E2=80=9Cconfirm the disposition of =E2=80=A63k lbs of tapes.=E2=80=9D IT =
Corp B responded: =E2=80=9CI can confirm that we did send this load of tape=
s for secure waste to energy incineration. Although that lot # is not the l=
ot # we used. They were processed =E2=80=98Confidential Material=E2=80=99 i=
n June of 2016.=E2=80=9D MSSB=E2=80=99s basis for believing that these tape=
s were in fact destroyed without any unauthorized access to customer PII an=
d consumer report information hinges on this email. MSSB has no other verif=
ication or 5 documentation that these tapes were destroyed.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In June 2021, MSSB obtained another fourteen o=
f the missing hard drives from a downstream purchaser. Based on forensic an=
alysis of these hard drives, thirteen of the devices contained a total of a=
t least 140,000 pieces of customer PII. The vast majority of the hard drive=
s from the 2016 Data Center Decommissioning remain missing. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">MSSB has identified an international shipping =
project that may have involved Moving Company. MSSB can state only that =E2=
=80=9Cdocuments suggest=E2=80=9D that Moving Company transported 18-36 unsp=
ecified devices to a storage location in New York City. It was contemplated=
 that those devices would be shipped internationally to Europe, potentially=
 by Moving Company.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Possibly thousands of ancient Morgan Stanley s=
torage devices, all over the globe, possibly full of customer data, possibl=
y useful for nefarious purposes. Probably not; there is no suggestion that =
anyone did anything at all nefarious with any of these things, and also not=
 much suggestion that you=C2=A0<em>could=C2=A0</em>do anything particularly=
 bad. (You=E2=80=99re not supposed to hand out customers=E2=80=99=C2=A0=E2=
=80=9Cpersonal identifying information=E2=80=9D to random hackers, but ther=
e=E2=80=99s no suggestion that it was, like, account passwords.)=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In recent years it has been popular for invest=
ment bank executives to say that they were becoming tech companies: They we=
re hiring developers, building apps, talking about big data. This is a good=
 thing to say, I suppose, insofar as being a tech company means that you ca=
n make large profits without putting too much of your own balance sheet at =
risk, that you can scale rapidly, that you can compete with Silicon Valley =
for employees.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But there is also an old-school understanding =
of investment banking in which it is a business of implicit knowledge, of p=
ersonal connections, that the value that a good banker can add can=E2=80=99=
t be reduced to an algorithm. There is a tension there. If banks are tech c=
ompanies then they will invest in good consistent electronic communication =
tools that create good searchable records. If banking is a business of pers=
onal connections, then bankers will ignore those tools and text clients on =
their personal cell phones because=C2=A0<em>that feels more personal</em>. =
Morgan Stanley <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/291=
11652.284535/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMD=
ctMTQvbW9yZ2FuLXN0YW5sZXktbWlzdXNlLW9mLXBlcnNvbmFsLWRldmljZXMtd2lsbC1jb3N0L=
TIwMC1taWxsaW9uP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA5MjAyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwm=
dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDkyMCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXl=
zdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB041c466f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  got fined $200 million=
 for that</a> this summer. You are supposed to use the official communicati=
on tools.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Similarly, if banking is a business of implici=
t knowledge, go ahead and throw out the old hard drives; what=E2=80=99s imp=
ortant is the knowledge in the hearts and minds of the bankers. If banking =
is a data business, you should probably wipe the hard drives before you thr=
ow them out.</p>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=

5px;">SPAC SPAC SPAC: Chamath</h2>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A special purpose acquisition company is a gam=
ble by its sponsors. The way a SPAC works is that the sponsors=C2=A0=E2=80=
=94=C2=A0often a private equity firm, but sometimes just a handful of rich =
individuals =E2=80=94 spend=C2=A0a few million dollars of their own money t=
o pay some startup costs for the SPAC. These fees pay for underwriters, law=
yers, accountants, registration fees, etc. And then the SPAC raises a few h=
undred million dollars from public investors, and the sponsors have two yea=
rs to=C2=A0find a company to take public with that money. If they find a ta=
rget, and the public investors approve the deal, the sponsors generally get=
 rich. In rough numbers they get shares of the target company worth about 2=
5% of the hundreds of millions of dollars that they raised from the public.=
 Their=C2=A0early investment of a few million dollars can turn into hundred=
s of millions of dollars of public-company stock. (Generally if they find a=
 deal the SPAC=E2=80=99s sponsors will invest more of their own money, so i=
t=E2=80=99s not quite this lucrative, but that is the essential nature of t=
he gamble.)=C2=A0It is helpful if they find a <em>good </em>target and the =
stock goes up, but the main thing is that if they do a deal at all they get=
 amply paid. If they don=E2=80=99t, they give the public investors their mo=
ney back and lose their startup investment.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The payoff there is incredibly asymmetric, and=
 so for a while in 2020 and early 2021 everyone with a reputation and a few=
 million dollars to spare piled into the SPAC business.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CYou a=
nd some buddies put up $500,000 each and then you get back $20 million each=
=E2=80=9D was a not entirely inaccurate pitch for sponsoring a SPAC. This l=
ed to a SPAC glut: There were more SPACs than good deals, the market got pi=
ckier, and now it is 2022 and a lot of SPACs look likely to reach their two=
-year deadline without a deal. Their sponsors will lose their wagers.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The King of SPACs was (is?) Chamath Palihapiti=
ya, the founder of Social Capital.=C2=A0He was early to the boom: He launch=
ed a SPAC with the clunky name Social Capital Hedosophia Holdings Corp., an=
d the punchy ticker IPOA,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important=
; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0back in 2017. IPOA <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness=
.com/click/29111652.284535/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9k=
YXRhLzE3MDY5NDYvMDAwMTE0NDIwNDE3MDQ4MzEyL3Y0NzUxNzJfNDI0YjQuaHRt/607f07ceef=
4b8524a319a23cB82b4cb48" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" tar=
get=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;"> raised $600 million</a> and used it to do=
 a merger with space company Virgin Galactic Holdings Inc.; Social Capital =
started by putting about $12 million into the SPAC to cover startup costs, =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A special purpose acquisition company is a gam=

for a while in late 2020 and early 2021 everyone with a reputation and a fe=

t of random rich people who saw their friends making money and wanted in on=
 the action. And then there was a SPAC glut, deals got scarce, and some num=

=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29111652.284535/aHR0cHM6L=

ation: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000=
000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> talked about thi=
s before</a>, and I confess that I find it kind of funny: Late-in-the-boom =
SPACs are the rare financial innovation that worked out fine for ordinary i=
nvestors (who get their money back with interest) but fleeced=C2=A0rich wel=
l-connected insiders.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One weird result of this is that if you are a =
late-in-the-boom SPAC sponsor you are probably rich but maybe not=C2=A0<em>=
that=C2=A0</em>rich, and while the payoff of a SPAC is very asymmetric, (1)=
 it looks increasingly distant and (2) you don=E2=80=99t want to throw good=
 money after bad. And SPACs have pretty thin balance sheets=C2=A0given thei=
r potential riches. So we have talked recently about Digital World Acquisit=
ion Corp., the $293 million SPAC that has signed a deal to take Trump Media=
 & Technology Group public. DWAC=E2=80=99s sponsors put up about $11.3 =
million to start the SPAC, and if the deal closes <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29111652.284535/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJ=

none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">their stake could be wor=
th</a>=C2=A0about $165 million. But the deal is still in limbo, due mostly =
to the US Securities and Exchange Commission=E2=80=99s review of the proxy =

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Executives behind a blank-cheque company that =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Digital World Acquisition Corporation, a speci=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">DWAC owes the New York-based firm a six-figure=
 sum but=C2=A0Orlando has informed it that there is no money to pay the bil=

ught on a new proxy solicitor, Alliance Advisors. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Despite an aggressive campaign by Orlando to r=
each DWAC=E2=80=99s base of retail investors, just over 40 per cent have vo=
ted in favour of the extension as of this week, according to a source famil=
iar with the count. The company requires 65 per cent of shareholders to app=
rove extending the deadline at its meeting on October 10.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Retail investors are notoriously difficult to =
reach and companies can often end up spending millions of dollars solicitin=
g their votes.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yeah I mean on the one hand you really do want=
 those votes; spending a few million dollars on proxy soliciting to make $1=
65 million feels like a good deal. On the other hand:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Even if you can drum up the retail vo=

=C2=A0</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">That proxy-solicitor money =
comes out of your own pocket; and</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=
Given that they only got 40% of the shareholders to vote, it=E2=80=99s not =
totally clear that Saratoga exactly earned its fees.</li>
</ol>

tock-market debut. Details of the listing could change depending on market =
conditions and other factors.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Instacart had previously leaned toward going p=
ublic through a direct listing, The Wall Street Journal previously reported=
.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In a direct listing, a company=E2=80=99s share=
s simply start trading on an exchange on a set day. There is a reference pr=
ice for where trading could start, but no shares are sold in advance at tha=
t price. Existing shareholders can sell their shares, but companies don=E2=
=80=99t raise any cash by going public.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yeah two years ago this would have been a dire=
ct listing. =E2=80=9CYour employees can just sell stock on the stock exchan=
ge without a big coordinated process; they=E2=80=99ll get the market-cleari=
ng price rather than having to sell at a discount so big institutions can g=
et an IPO pop.=E2=80=9D Now it=E2=80=99s a normal IPO.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One possible story here is that direct listing=
s and SPACs are solutions to the bull-market problem that <a href=3D"https:=
//link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29111652.284535/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmx=
vb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTktMTAtMDQvaG93LXRvLWRpc3J1cHQtdG=
hlLWlwby1wb3A_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDkyMDIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1d=
G1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIwOTIwJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0=
dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB343dd2f8" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D=
"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  IPOs mostly go up</a>. If=
 you price an IPO at $20 and the stock trades up to $30 in a day, you =E2=
=80=9Cleft money on the table.=E2=80=9D If every week 10 companies price IP=
Os that trade up 50%, and you want to go public, you will start thinking ab=
out ways to avoid leaving money on the table. Selling stock in a direct lis=
ting =E2=80=94=C2=A0where you get the market-clearing price in an auction, =
not a price negotiated with your underwriters based on stated demand from b=
ig institutions =E2=80=94=C2=A0is a way to do that. A <a href=3D"https://li=
nk.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/29111652.284535/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21=
iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDItMjMvdGVzbGEtcml2YWwtbHVjaWQtbW=
90b3JzLXBhaWQtYS1wcmljZS1mb3ItaXRzLXNwYWMtZGVhbD9jbXBpZD1CQkQwOTIwMjJfTU9OR=
VlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0y=
MjA5MjAmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB317ddb75" =
itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"c=
olor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; =
border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color:=
 #ccc;">  SPAC is not</a>, particularly, but it was marketed that way anywa=
y.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But when the stock market is down and volatili=
ty is up, SPACs and direct listings are less appealing. The IPO pop =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0the propensity of most IPOs to trade up on the first day =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0pays for certainty. =E2=80=9CWe bring you IPOs every week and they us=
ually go up,=E2=80=9D banks implicitly say to their investor clients, =E2=
=80=9Cso if you like that service we expect you to buy the IPOs that don=E2=
=80=99t go up too.=E2=80=9D If you sell stock in a direct listing, nobody h=
as to buy it. If you sell stock in an IPO, they =E2=80=A6=C2=A0well, nobody=
=C2=A0<em>has=C2=A0</em>to buy it, but it is encouraged.<a class=3D"footnot=
e-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; =
display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a>=C2=A0In a down market you don=E2=80=99t have to worry about the proble=
m of IPOs going up too much; you have to worry about actually getting your =
money. The IPO might be a better way of doing that than the alternatives.</=
p>

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Amid the wreckage of the great bond-market bus=
t of 2022, there is a silver lining for some: Traders in the trenches of th=
e $24 trillion Treasury market say it=E2=80=99s finally exciting again. ...=
</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Of course in finance, the levels of excitement=
 is usually tightly correlated with the amount of money being made. So it=
=E2=80=99s no surprise the two are rising in tandem. Trading revenues have =
boomed for the major US banks with a strong presence in US rates markets. A=
t Citadel Securities, an important provider of prices in the Treasury marke=
t, this year=E2=80=99s turbulence led to a record $4.2 billion in first-hal=
f net trading revenue.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWe are right in the sweet spot of rat=
es really being an interesting market, with clients being excited to trade,=
=E2=80=9D said Paul Hamill, head of global fixed income, currencies and com=
modities distribution at Citadel Securities. =E2=80=9CEveryone is spending =
all day talking to clients and talking to each other. It=E2=80=99s been fun=
.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I recommend taking that quote as literally as =
possible. The head of fixed income at Citadel Securities is like =E2=80=9Co=
h I get to talk to my friends all day, it=E2=80=99s so fun.=E2=80=9D How ni=
ce for him.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We have spent years around here talking about =
how people were worried about bond market liquidity: People who wanted to b=
uy and sell bonds had a hard time doing it, because banks and other market =
makers were not willing to commit a lot of capital. This worry was often ex=
pressed as a hypothetical: Sure, things look fine now because the market is=
 quiet, but in times of volatility the lack of liquidity will be a problem.=
 But of course times of volatility are when market makers can make a lot of=
 money. Which makes them more willing to commit capital, because they are h=
aving fun.</p>
